2025;Understanding Privacy Norms through Web Forms;Hao Cui
column3: and Athina Markopoulou;Web forms are one of the primary ways to collect personal information online
column4: yet they are relatively under-studied. Unlike web tracking
column5: data collection through web forms is explicit and contextualized. Users (i) are asked to input specific personal information types
column6: and (ii) know the specific context (i.e.
column7: on which website and for what purpose). For web forms to be trusted by users
column8: they must meet the common sense standards of appropriate data collection practices within a particular context (i.e.
column9: privacy norms). In this paper
column10: we extract the privacy norms embedded within web forms through a measurement study. First
column11: we build a specialized crawler to discover web forms on websites. We run it on 11
column12: 500 popular websites
column13: and we create a dataset of 293K web forms. Second
column14: to process data of this scale
column15: we develop a cost-efficient way to annotate web forms with form types and personal information types
column16: using text classifiers trained with assistance of large language models (LLMs). Third
column18: we reveal common patterns of data collection practices. We find that (i) these patterns are explained by functional necessities and legal obligations
column20: and that (ii) deviations from the observed norms often signal unnecessary data collection. In addition
column21: we analyze the privacy policies that accompany web forms. We show that
column22: despite their wide adoption and use
column23: there is a disconnect between privacy policy disclosures and the observed privacy norms.;Web form

column6: and Athina Markopoulou;Virtual reality (VR) platforms enable a wide range of applications

column13: and facial expression). The data from these newly available sensors can be used to uniquely identify a user
column14: even in the absence of explicit identifiers. In this paper
column15: we seek to understand the extent to which a user can be identified based solely on VR sensor data
column16: within and across real-world apps from diverse genres. We consider adversaries with capabilities that range from observing APIs available within a single app (app adversary) to observing all or selected sensor measurements across multiple apps on the VR device (device adversary). To that end
column18: a framework for collecting and analyzing data from all sensor groups collected by multiple apps running on a VR device. We use BehaVR to collect data from real users that interact with 20 popular real-world apps. We use that data to build machine learning models for user identification within and across apps

column1: 2025;Topology-Based Reconstruction Prevention for Decentralised Learning;Florine W. Dekker
column3: and Mauro Conti;"Decentralised learning has recently gained traction as an alternative to federated learning in which both data and coordination are distributed over its users. To preserve data confidentiality
column4: decentralised learning relies on differential privacy
column5: multi-party computation
column6: or a combination thereof. However
column7: running multiple privacy-preserving summations in sequence may allow adversaries to perform reconstruction attacks. Unfortunately
column8: current reconstruction countermeasures either cannot trivially be adapted to the distributed setting
column9: or add excessive amounts of noise.

column1: In this work
column2: we first show that passive honest-but-curious adversaries can infer other users' private data after several privacy-preserving summations. For example
column4: we show that only three passive honest-but-curious adversaries succeed at reconstructing private data 11.0% of the time
column5: requiring an average of 8.8 summations per adversary. The success rate depends only on the adversaries' direct neighbourhood
column6: independent of the size of the full network. We consider weak adversaries
column7: who do not control the graph topology and can exploit neither the inner workings of the summation protocol nor the specifics of users' data.

column1: We develop a mathematical understanding of how reconstruction relates to topology and propose the first topology-based decentralised defence against reconstruction attacks. Specifically
column2: we show that reconstruction requires a number of adversaries linear in the length of the network's shortest cycle. Consequently
column3: reconstructing private data from privacy-preserving summations is impossible in acyclic networks.

column1: Our work is a stepping stone for a formal theory of topology-based reconstruction defences. Such a theory would generalise our countermeasure beyond summation
column2: define confidentiality in terms of entropy
column3: and describe the effects of differential privacy.";

column1: 2025;Mastic: Private Weighted Heavy-Hitters and Attribute-Based Metrics;Dimitris Mouris
column4: Pratik Sarkar
column5: and Nektarios Georgios Tsoutsos;Insight into user experience and behavior is critical to the success of large software systems and web services. Gaining such insights
column6: while preserving user privacy
column7: is a significant challenge. Recent advancements in multi-party computation have made it practical to securely compute aggregates over secret shared data. Two such protocols have emerged as candidates for standardization at the IETF: Prio (NSDI 2017) for general-purpose statistics─ and Poplar (IEEE S&P 2021) for heavy hitters
column8: where the goal is to compute the most popular inputs held by users without learning the inputs themselves. While each of these protocols is well-suited to certain applications
column9: there remain a number of use cases identified by IETF for which neither Prio nor Poplar is practical. We introduce Mastic
column10: a protocol for the following functionality: each of a large number of clients holds an input (e.g.
column11: a URL) and its corresponding weight (e.g.
column12: page load time)─ for a given candidate input (or prefix)
column13: a small number of non-colluding servers wish to securely aggregate the weights of clients that hold that input (or some input with that prefix)
column14: without learning the weights or which client holds which input. This functionality makes two new classes of applications possible. The first is a natural generalization of heavy hitters we call weighted heavy-hitters. The second is an enhancement of Prio-style metrics we call attribute-based metrics in which aggregates are grouped by hierarchical user attributes (e.g.
column15: their geographic location or software version). We demonstrate Mastic’s practicality for these applications with a real-world example of each. We also compare our protocol with Prio and Poplar on a wide area network. Overall
column16: we report over one order of magnitude performance improvement over Poplar for plain heavy-hitters and 1.5 − 2× improvement over Prio for attribute-based metrics.;Distributed point functions

column8: we propose and discuss a simple yet effective solution: the application should enable direct modification of locally stored messages in the user interface. This position paper raises several unanswered questions

column12: and prove it to be stronger than the INDCDP and SIM-CDP and incomparable to SIM+-CDP. We demonstrate the usability of the SIM∗ -CDP definition by showing how to satisfy it by the use of an available distributed protocol for sampling truncated geometric noise. Further

column1: 2025;SoK: Computational and Distributed Differential Privacy for MPC;Fredrik Meisingseth and Christian Rechberger;In the last fifteen years

column1: 2025;MixBuy: Contingent Payment in the Presence of Coin Mixers;Diego Castejon-Molina
column3: and Pedro Moreno-Sanchez;A contingent payment protocol involves two mutually distrustful parties
column4: a buyer and a seller
column5: operating on the same blockchain
column6: and a digital product
column7: whose ownership is not tracked on a blockchain (e.g. a digital book
column8: but not a NFT). The buyer holds coins on the blockchain and transfers them to the seller in exchange for the product. However
column9: if the blockchain does not hide transaction details

column11: we take contingent payment a step further: we consider a buyer who wishes to buy a digital product from a seller routing the payment via an untrusted mixer. Crucially
column12: we require that said payment is unlinkable
column13: meaning that the mixer (or any other observer) does not learn which buyer is paying which seller. We refer to such setting as unlinkable contingent payment (UCP). We present MixBuy
column14: a system that realizes UCP. Mixbuy relies on oracle-based unlinkable contingent payment (O-UCP)
column15: a novel fourparty cryptographic protocol where the mixer pays the seller and the seller provides the buyer with the product only if a semi-trusted notary attests that the buyer has paid the mixer. More specifically
column16: we require four security notions: (i) mixer security that guarantees that if the mixer pays the seller
column17: the intermediary must get paid from the buyer─ (ii) seller security that guarantees that if the seller delivers the product to the buyer
column18: the seller must get paid from the intermediary─ (iii) buyer security that guarantees that if the buyer pays the intermediary
column19: the buyer must obtain the product─ and (iv) unlinkability that guarantees that given a set of buyers and sellers
column20: the intermediary should not learn which buyer paid which seller. We present a provably secure and efficient cryptographic construction for O-UCP. Our construction can be readily used to realize UCP on most blockchains
column21: as it has minimal functionality requirements (i.e.

column4: Hayes and Danezis proposed the use of guard sets
column5: in which the Tor system groups all guards into sets
column6: and each client picks one of these sets and uses its guards. Unfortunately
column7: guard sets frequently need nodes added or they are broken up due to fluctuations in network bandwidth. In this paper
column8: we first show that these breakups create opportunities for malicious guards to join many guard sets by merely tuning the bandwidth they make available to Tor
column9: and this greatly increases the number of clients exposed to malicious guards. To address this problem
column10: we propose a new method for forming guard sets based on Internet location. We construct a hierarchy that keeps clients and guards together more reliably and prevents guards from easily joining arbitrary guard sets. This approach also has the advantage of confining an attacker with access to limited locations on the Internet to a small number of guard sets. We simulate this guard set design using historical Tor data in the presence of both relay-level adversaries and networklevel adversaries
column11: and we find that our approach is good at confining the adversary into few guard sets
column12: thus limiting the impact of attacks.;Tor network
